species: Mt. Rainier Wind Above Treeline -- ultra-high-velocity howl
sound_type: Ultra-high-velocity howl: unmediated atmospheric turbulence over rock, ice, and snow above treeline. Dominant: broadband 500-8000 Hz, peak energy 1000-4000 Hz. Sustained 85-95 dB; gust peaks 95+ dB. Duration: continuous during storm events (hours to days). No Aeolian tones from tree structures. No structural resonance from trunks. Pure atmosphere.
ecosystem: Summit zone, Mt. Rainier (Tahoma), above treeline 6000-7000 ft. Camp Muir 10188 ft. Summit 14411 ft. 25 named glaciers, 35 square miles ice. Topographically most prominent peak in contiguous US. Pacific storm interception without upstream terrain reduction.
season: Storm (year-round above treeline; peak intensity winter). Pacific cyclones produce 60-100+ mph sustained at summit zone. Orographic enhancement: 80-100 mph at sea level → 120-150 mph at summit cone during extreme events. Paradise (5400 ft): 100+ mph measured multiple times.
milestone: HIGHEST-ALTITUDE METEOROLOGICAL ENTRY IN THE ENGINE. 14,411 ft. ACOUSTIC BOUNDARY DOCUMENTED (consecutive with 326 Douglas Fir Gale): below treeline = mediated, structured moan-roar (E=7); above treeline = unmediated, unstructured howl (E=7). Same energy, categorically different acoustic character. Three Rainier entries in engine: 261 (volcanic tremor) + 262 (lahar) + 327 (wind). Tahoma: Lushootseed language, Puyallup and Nisqually peoples.

coordinates.description: Mt. Rainier summit: 46.853°N 121.726°W. Camp Muir: 46.835°N 121.732°W, 10,188 ft, windward south face. Treeline transition: 46.79°N 121.74°W area, approximately 6,000-7,000 ft elevation.

acoustic_data.frequency: 500-8000 Hz dominant (broadband turbulence). Peak energy 1000-4000 Hz. Sub-bass turbulent eddies (10-100 Hz) felt but typically below hearing threshold. Glacier surface wind: high-frequency hiss from turbulence over acoustically smooth ice.
acoustic_data.amplitude: 85-95 dB sustained at surface (60-80 mph). Gust peaks: 95+ dB. Higher than below-treeline Douglas fir gale (70-85 dB at 20m) because unmediated -- full atmospheric energy at measurement point.
acoustic_data.character: Omnidirectional: wind comes from specific direction but reflects off mountain topography creating complex source distribution. Continuous rather than gust-pulsed (contrast with forest gust cycle 5-15 sec). No Aeolian tones (no appropriate tree branch structures). Camp Muir guide wires add Aeolian whistles 1000-4000 Hz.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7 through unmediated atmospheric force at extreme altitude. Same energy as Douglas fir gale (326) through different mechanism: mediated (tree architecture) vs. unmediated (pure atmosphere). Both E=7 but qualitatively distinct.

nodes.title: Treeline as Acoustic Boundary -- 326+327 Together
nodes.description: Consecutive degrees (326 below treeline, 327 above treeline) document the Pacific Northwest's most important acoustic boundary: vegetation-mediated vs. unmediated atmospheric sound. Same energy (E=7 both). Different character: structured moan-roar (Douglas fir) vs. unstructured howl (above treeline). No other pair of consecutive SPS degrees documents a single acoustic phenomenon before and after its primary mediating structure.

nodes.title: Unmediated Atmospheric Wind -- Pure Atmosphere
nodes.description: Above treeline: no canopy, no needle aerodynamics, no trunk flex, no Aeolian tones from branch structures. The wind interacts with rock, ice, snow, and human structures (guide wires, tents). The acoustic output is pure atmospheric turbulence. The forest's acoustic architecture is absent.

nodes.title: Three Rainier Entries: 261+262+327
nodes.description: Degrees 261 (volcanic tremor), 262 (lahar simulation), and 327 (wind above treeline) document Mt. Rainier's three extreme character dimensions: geological, hazard, and meteorological. The mountain appears three times because it is three different extreme things simultaneously.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 below treeline. Consecutive degrees documenting the acoustic boundary. Mediated (tree architecture) vs. unmediated (pure atmosphere). Same energy, different world.
Rainier's geological character. 327 is the meteorological complement.
Rainier's hazard character. 261+262+327 = all three Rainier extreme dimensions.
Lower-elevation landscape wind event. 327 is high-altitude, summit-zone, unmediated.
E=6 + E=7 = E=13. Capitol Hill intimacy vs. Pacific Northwest's most exposed meteorological location.
